summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 12 and iPhone 12 Pro, focusing on how their industrial design represents a significant shift: despite having 6.1-inch displays (same as the larger iPhone 11), they feel like the smaller iPhone 11 Pro in hand thanks to reduced bezels and flat sides. He argues Apple designs around a target device size, not display size, and that the iPhone 12 is the real star — equally nice as the Pro but lighter and cheaper. He covers MagSafe (charming but not a dock replacement), 5G (mmWave is astonishingly fast but practically useless for most people), and the A14's continued dominance over competitors.
key_points: Display size no longer corresponds to device size: the 6.1-inch iPhone 12/12 Pro feel like the old 5.8-inch 11 Pro, not the 6.1-inch iPhone 11
The iPhone 12 is just as nice as the 12 Pro in every way except the camera system, making the Pro's $120 premium hard to justify for non-photographers
Apple designs iPhones around a target device size and feel in hand, then fits the largest possible display into that form factor
5G mmWave delivers stunning 1,200-2,700 Mbps speeds but is outdoor-only, block-by-block coverage that has no practical everyday use yet
MagSafe is best understood as a magnetic cable replacement, not a charging pad — delightful but incompatible with Apple Watch, a missed opportunity
money_quotes: Display size is no longer a proximate metric for relative iPhone device size.
I don't think they start with a display size and then design a phone around that. I think they start with a device size and then fit a display into that design.
The iPhone XR and 11 were compromise designs.
Camera system aside, the iPhone 12 is just as nice as the iPhone 12 Pro, and it costs $120 less for the same amount of storage.
Watches are jewelry, but phones are tools.
If you have your heart set on this vibrant blue iPhone 12, nothing else will do — you'll wait until it's back in stock or trek to another store.
I greatly look forward to never again waking up with my phone in the red because I left it ever so slightly misaligned on my bedside Qi charger overnight.
5G may well be faster than LTE, but allowing more data over cellular should depend on your plan's data cap, not the speed of the connection.
key_insight: The iPhone 12 represents a quiet design triumph — fitting a larger display into a smaller body — while the Pro's premium buys you cameras but not a meaningfully nicer phone.
